specification.topic-statement: The input buffer stores UTF-8 text entered by the user, tracks a cursor position within that text, and computes the display layout needed to render the text correctly across wrapped terminal lines.

scope.in-scope: Text storage and growth, codepoint insertion and deletion, cursor position as a dual byte-and-grapheme representation, word-level deletion, line-level navigation and deletion, multi-line vertical movement with column preservation, display width calculation with ANSI sequence exclusion, physical-line count calculation, and layout cache invalidation.
scope.boundary: How the input buffer's text and cursor position are drawn to the terminal is handled by the input layer renderer and is out of scope here.
What user input triggers which editing operation is decided by the action dispatcher and is out of scope here.

field.name: text
field.note: UTF-8 encoded character data; all inserted content is valid UTF-8

field.name: length
field.note: number of bytes currently in the text array, not counting any null terminator

field.name: cursor byte offset
field.note: byte position of the cursor within the text array; authoritative for text manipulation

field.name: cursor grapheme offset
field.note: count of Unicode grapheme clusters from the start of the text to the cursor; authoritative for display and navigation semantics

field.name: target column
field.note: grapheme column preserved across vertical cursor movements; reset to zero by any horizontal movement

field.name: layout dirty
field.note: true when the cached physical line count is stale and must be recomputed

field.name: cached physical lines
field.note: number of terminal rows the text occupies at the cached terminal width; valid only when layout is not dirty

field.name: cached terminal width
field.note: terminal column width used for the most recent physical-line calculation

behavior.text-storage: Text is stored in a dynamically allocated byte array. The array grows as characters are inserted; it never shrinks. All text stored in the buffer is guaranteed to be valid UTF-8 — the insert operations encode each codepoint to UTF-8 before appending, so no runtime validation is performed on reads.
behavior.insertion: A Unicode codepoint is inserted at the current cursor position by encoding it to its 1–4 byte UTF-8 representation and inserting those bytes at the cursor's byte offset. A newline character is inserted as a single byte. After insertion, the cursor advances by the number of bytes inserted, the cursor's grapheme offset is resynchronized, and the layout cache is invalidated.
behavior.backspace-deletion: Backspace deletes the complete UTF-8 character immediately before the cursor. The operation scans backward from the cursor's byte position to find the boundary of the preceding character (skipping UTF-8 continuation bytes), removes all of that character's bytes, and moves the cursor to where the deleted character began. If the cursor is at position zero, backspace is a no-op. The layout cache is invalidated on any deletion.
behavior.forward-deletion: Forward delete removes the complete UTF-8 character at the cursor position. The character's byte length is determined by its leading byte, and all bytes are removed. The cursor does not move — it now points to the character that immediately followed the deleted one. If the cursor is at or past the end of the text, this is a no-op.
behavior.word-deletion:
Word deletion removes the word immediately before the cursor, implementing standard Ctrl+W semantics. The operation:
1. Skips whitespace bytes backward from the cursor. 2. Identifies the character class of the first non-whitespace byte (alphanumeric, or punctuation / other). 3. Deletes backward through all consecutive bytes of the same class.
Character classes are: alphanumeric (ASCII letters and digits), whitespace (space, tab, newline, carriage return), and all other bytes (including multi-byte UTF-8 leading bytes, which are treated as alphanumeric for this purpose). The cursor is moved to the deletion boundary.
behavior.cursor-representation:
The cursor is maintained as two synchronized values: a byte offset for text manipulation and a grapheme offset for display and navigation. The byte offset is the primary value for all text operations. After any operation that changes the byte offset, the grapheme offset is resynchronized by counting Unicode grapheme clusters from the start of the buffer to the new byte position.
Grapheme cluster boundaries are determined by Unicode standard grapheme break rules. A base character and its combining diacritics form a single grapheme; independent characters each form their own grapheme.
behavior.horizontal-cursor-movement: Moving the cursor left positions it at the immediately preceding grapheme boundary. Moving right positions it at the next grapheme boundary. Both directions are no-ops at their respective boundaries (start and end of text). Either movement resets the target column to zero.
behavior.line-navigation:
The buffer supports navigation to logical line boundaries defined by newline characters:
- **Line start**: moves the cursor to the byte position immediately after the preceding newline, or to zero for the first line. - **Line end**: moves the cursor to the byte position of the next newline, or to the text length if none follows. The newline itself is excluded from the position.
Both operations reset the target column.
Positioning at line end places the cursor before the newline, not after it. Pressing right from line end moves to the next line's start.
behavior.vertical-cursor-movement:
Moving the cursor up or down preserves the user's intended column across lines of differing lengths.
On the first vertical movement, the current grapheme column is stored as the target column. Subsequent vertical movements aim for that stored target column in the destination line. If the destination line is shorter than the target column, the cursor is placed at the line end.
Any horizontal movement (left, right, line-start, line-end, or word deletion) resets the target column to zero, so the next vertical movement establishes a fresh target.
behavior.line-deletion:
Two line-deletion operations are supported:
- **Kill line**: deletes the entire current line, including its newline character. The cursor moves to where the line began. If the buffer becomes empty, the cursor is at position zero. - **Kill to end of line**: deletes from the cursor to (but not including) the newline. If the cursor is already at line end, this is a no-op.
Both operations invalidate the layout cache.
behavior.layout-calculation:
The buffer computes how many physical (wrapped) terminal rows its text occupies at a given terminal width. The calculation:
1. Splits the text at newline characters into logical lines. 2. For each logical line, computes the display width by walking the UTF-8 text and summing per-codepoint display widths (0, 1, or 2 columns) while skipping ANSI escape sequences entirely. 3. For each logical line, computes the physical row count as the ceiling of display width divided by terminal width, with a minimum of one row per logical line. 4. Sums the per-line physical row counts.
An empty buffer (no text) has zero physical lines.
ANSI color and formatting sequences embedded in the text are skipped during display-width measurement so they do not inflate the computed row count.
behavior.layout-cache:
The physical line count is cached alongside the terminal width at which it was computed. The cache is invalidated whenever text is modified (set dirty). On the next call that requires the layout, if the cache is dirty or the terminal width has changed, the layout is recomputed and the cache is updated.
Callers that change the terminal width should recompute after resizing; the cache detects the mismatch automatically.
behavior.clear-operation: Clearing the buffer sets the text length to zero, resets the cursor to position zero, resets the target column, and invalidates the layout cache. Allocated memory is not released — the buffer retains its capacity for future input.
behavior.error-handling: Memory allocation failures during text buffer growth cause an immediate process abort. Navigation and editing operations at boundaries (cursor at start or end, empty buffer) are silent no-ops and never return errors.
